options.C: Ns produce violent precipitation, while Cb produce continuous rain.

options.A: When convection sets in and wind increases with altitude.

options.C: Rotor clouds.
Several kilometers around it.

options.B: They create surface breezes or gust fronts, making approach and landing difficult.

q012.question: Clouds favorable for performance flights are:

options.C: Cumulus clouds.
options.D: Cirrocumulus, nimbostratus, and stratocumulus clouds.

options.C: Stratosphere.

q014.question: In meteorology, an air mass is characterized by:

options.C: The vertical distribution of temperature.

options.C: Humidity.

options.C: Mixes with cold air.

options.C: Evaporation/condensation and turbulence.

q022.question: The sun mainly heats the atmosphere:

options.C: Indirectly through the ground.
Radiation.

q024.question: We call downslope breeze or katabatic breeze:

options.C: A wind that, as a consequence of convection, descends along a slope.

options.D: Increase the risk of entering a stabilized deep stall.

q182.question: In flight, hands-up position corresponds to:

options.C: Maximum glide ratio through the air.
options.D: Maximum speed.

options.A: The wing loading, the glide ratio over the ground.
options.B: Maximum glide ratio, minimum sink rate, penetration.
options.C: Handling and behavior in turbulence.
options.D: Stability.

q184.question: For your safety, have the wing checked by the manufacturer when you notice:

options.C: Damaged seams.

q185.question: Your new wing systematically turns to one side, with no brake input and with the brake lines fully released:

options.A: You lengthen the brake line on the opposite side.
options.B: You modify the riser level in the harness.
options.C: You loosen the leg strap on the opposite side.
options.D: You contact the dealer for a factory check and/or consult an instructor or experienced pilot.

options.C: Makes the wing slow to inflate.
options.D: Makes the wing more nervous on takeoff.

options.A: Increases the penetration of the wing.
options.C: Makes the wing slower to inflate.
options.D: Makes the wing more nervous on takeoff.

q188.question: In-flight trim adjustment systems (trims, speed systems):

options.C: Must be used with caution in turbulence, especially while adjusting.
options.D: Must be easy to disengage in flight.

q189.question: During storage and transport, the paraglider must be protected from proximity to or contact with:

options.C: Humidity.
options.D: The sun.

q190.question: Name the factors that age a wing:

options.C: Turbulence.

options.A: May prevent the wing from rising during inflation.
options.B: Reduce the possibility of building speed in flight if needed.
options.C: Increase the risk of stall.
options.D: Are favorable, especially for beginners.

q192.question: Each line withstands a load of approximately:

options.C: With polyester tapes.
Has good tensile strength, but is weak in shear and bending.
options.D: Must be sheathed or protected with tape for effective protection against abrasion and ultraviolet rays.

options.C: Has a low melting temperature (significant risk of breakage from rubbing).
options.D: Has good tensile strength.

options.B: May reduce tensile strength by up to 50%.
options.C: Does not affect tensile strength.

options.A: Promote the practice of competitive free flight.
options.C: Advance equipment technology.
options.D: Offer a French certification for free-flight wings.

q198.question: The certification sticker on a paraglider:

options.A: Is not mandatory on all units of a certified model.
options.B: Gives indications about that model’s behavior in certain flight configurations.
options.C: Gives an idea of the piloting level required for that model: beginner, intermediate, etc.
options.D: Indicates what type of harness was used for the tests.

options.A: Certifies that any certified paraglider can be used without risk by any pilot, including beginners.
options.B: Gives an idea of that model's behavior.
options.C: Requires the manufacturer to keep the same technical characteristics in all produced units.
options.D: Is valid regardless of the harness used with the wing.

options.C: An aircraft not subject to registration.
options.D: A parachute.

options.A: UL gliders have priority over sailplanes.
options.B: UL gliders must give way to sailplanes.
options.C: UL gliders have priority over towed combinations, such as airplane + sailplane, ultralight + hang glider, ultralight + advertising banner, etc.

options.A: The hang glider must give way to the paraglider.
options.B: The paraglider must give way to the hang glider.
options.C: Hang gliders and paragliders must follow the same right-of-way rules in the air.

q203.question: A wing is coming head-on toward you; you perform:

options.A: A turn to the left; the other pilot also turns left.
options.B: A turn to the right; the other pilot also turns right.
options.C: A descent (the other remains at the same level).

q204.question: In flight, an ultralight glider approaches from your right on a converging course:

options.C: Right of way depends on the size of the aircraft.

q205.question: Far from terrain, you are flying the same course and at the same altitude as a slower wing. You overtake it on:

options.C: The right.

q206.question: Flying at the same altitude, right-of-way belongs to:

options.A: The wing that approaches from the right.
options.B: The tandem over the solo wing.
options.C: The wing that arrives first at the lift.
options.D: The wing piloted by a beginner.

q207.question: In ridge soaring, right of way belongs to the wing that:

options.A: Has the slope/ridge on its right.
options.B: Has the slope/ridge on its left.
options.C: Is located on the windward side of the terrain.
options.D: Is located on the leeward side of the terrain.

q208.question: You are ridge soaring; the terrain is on your left. A wing approaches head-on at the same altitude:

options.A: The other pilot has right of way.
options.B: You have priority and maintain your heading.
options.C: You try to pass below it.

q209.question: Two wings are following each other close to terrain located on their right. The second wing is faster:

options.A: It must overtake on the left.
options.B: It must overtake on the right, between the terrain and the other wing.
options.C: Turns around.

q210.question: You are the first to enter a thermal/lift:

options.A: Wings entering afterward must adopt your turning direction.
options.B: A wing entering the lift at the same level as you must turn 180 degrees and in the same direction.
options.C: You have right of way over a wing below you that is catching up to you.
options.D: No other wing has the right to use that lift.

q211.question: You want to enter a thermal/lift while another wing is turning 50 m below:

options.A: You turn in the same direction as the other wing.
options.B: Turn in the opposite direction so you can keep watching it.
options.C: Wait for it to pass overhead before entering, otherwise the peripheral sink may bring you to the same altitude.

options.A: More than 300 meters above any terrain.
options.B: More than 900 meters above sea level.
options.C: Nowhere.

q213.question: Flying over isolated obstacles must be done with a minimum vertical and horizontal margin of:

options.C: 150 meters.
options.D: 200 meters.

q214.question: Flying over built-up areas or a gathering of people must be done with a minimum margin of:

options.C: 300 meters vertically and 600 meters horizontally.

options.A: To keep sufficient distance to prevent collisions.
options.B: To turn right when crossing at the same altitude.
options.C: To pass a slower wing on the left.
options.D: To turn in the same direction as the first wing entering the thermal lift.

options.C: The Ministry of the Interior.
options.D: Every pilot.

q217.question: Applying the rule of addition is:

options.A: Avoid accumulating stress factors that, separately, do not lead to accidents but, when combined, make the risk unacceptable.
options.B: Assess the risks and avoid adding them together.
options.C: Identify the new elements in a situation and accept no more than one at a time.

options.A: Is due to a deficit of oxygen in the blood.
options.B: Is due to cooling of the atmosphere.
options.C: May appear as a feeling of well-being.
options.D: Causes impaired judgment and reduced muscular coordination.

options.A: The first symptoms may appear around 2,000 m above sea level.
options.C: May impair judgment.
options.D: Has different effects on each pilot.

q220.question: Factors that aggravate the risk of hypoxia:

options.C: A high-fat diet.
options.D: A pilot who is not warmly dressed.

q221.question: When anticipating the possibility of gaining a lot of altitude or making a long flight, it is advisable to equip yourself with:

options.C: Good footwear, warm clothing, gloves, sunglasses, and drinking water.

q222.question: In strong wind, tethered flight over a fixed point, with a rope tied to the ground:

options.C: Is discouraged.
options.D: May cause situations in which the wing becomes uncontrollable.

q223.question: When establishing a flight plan, take into account:

options.A: The location of takeoff and landing areas.
options.C: Wind aloft.
options.D: Valley wind.

q224.question: To take off safely, it is better to choose a slope:

options.C: Without obstacles.

q225.question: The slope of a takeoff area must be:

options.A: Less than or equal to the slope corresponding to the wing’s maximum glide ratio.
options.B: Greater than the slope corresponding to the wing's maximum glide ratio.
options.C: It should not be compared with the wing's glide angle.

q226.question: You are preparing to take off at 3 p.m. from a sunny slope where a light mountain breeze has established. There is no meteorological wind:

options.A: The wind in the valley will be calm or very light.
options.B: You must anticipate the possibility of strong wind in the valley, especially if it is narrow.
options.C: In all cases, it is prudent to plan alternative landing options, taking into account possible strong wind in the valley.

q227.question: Your takeoff faces west and the weather is good. To find the best launch conditions, it is better to take off:

options.B: The time of day does not matter.
options.C: At the beginning of the afternoon.

q228.question: Your takeoff faces east and the weather is good. To find the best conditions, it is better to take off:

options.B: The time of day does not matter.
options.C: During the afternoon.

q229.question: You are in the mountains, there is no wind, and according to your wing’s glide ratio you would just reach the landing field. You:

options.C: Fly at midday to take advantage of lift.
options.D: Ground effect will help you during landing.

q230.question: A 40-meter-thick stratus layer is located below the takeoff:

options.A: 40 m is not much; you take off.
options.B: You wait for it to clear; otherwise, you do not take off.
options.C: If there is enough wind for ridge soaring, you take off and stay above the layer.

q231.question: Compared with the wind measured at takeoff 2 meters above the ground, there is a risk that 30 meters higher it will be:

options.C: Weaker.
A light crosswind is not a problem.
options.D: You look for a better-oriented takeoff.

q233.question: During the takeoff run, if the slope angle increases and the trim setting does not change, the angle of attack:

options.C: Increases.

q234.question: During the takeoff run, your angle of attack is the angle between the plane of the wing and:

options.C: The horizontal.

q235.question: During takeoff the wind is from behind. The chances of failing to take off are due to:

options.B: To the effects of wind gradient.
options.C: The high running speed you would need to reach.

q236.question: At takeoff there is 20 km/h of wind on the slope; your wing starts flying at 30 km/h. Along your takeoff path, you must run at:

options.C: 30 km/h.
options.D: 40 km/h.

q237.question: In high mountains, air density decreases with altitude; for my wing to take off, I must run:

options.C: For a longer time.
options.D: For a shorter time.

q238.question: After a takeoff run in which your harness skims the ground, you barely manage to lift off:

options.A: You are certainly in a fast-flight regime; you may slow down to enter minimum sink.
options.B: You are in slow flight and must skim the terrain while building speed to transition to normal flight.
options.C: You are probably in danger; if you do not immediately build airspeed, you may fall.

q239.question: A friend has just taken off, and you catch up with him to fly together:

options.A: It is dangerous because you will encounter the turbulence from his wing.
options.B: It is the only way to take good photos.
options.C: It is delicate but possible.

q240.question: The turbulence produced by another wing is dangerous:

options.B: Over several dozen meters behind it along its air path.
options.C: Because it can cause collapses in your own wing.

q241.question: The turbulence from one wing is dangerous for another aircraft located:

options.C: Below.
options.D: In the flight path/air.

q242.question: Flying while making sure there is a landing area is:

options.A: Have enough range to reach it at all times, regardless of the aerological conditions encountered.
options.B: Stay over the usual site without flying cross-country.

q243.question: If the meteorological wind changes direction or strength during the flight, it is advisable to:

options.A: Change the flight plan in time to have a better chance of a safe landing.
options.B: Immediately head toward the landing field and return at any cost.
options.C: Slow down as much as possible to delay the landing, which will be delicate.
options.D: Immediately locate alternate landing fields to prepare for the possibility of an emergency landing.

q244.question: A wing flying into wind moves forward relative to the ground. The wind is:

options.C: Cannot be known.

q245.question: You are flying with a tailwind. Your cruising airspeed is equal to the meteorological wind speed. The ground:

options.A: Moves past twice as fast as with no wind.
options.B: Moves past twice as slowly as with no wind.
options.C: Appears stationary.

q246.question: The speed of your aircraft relative to the ground is:

options.C: Never changes.

q247.question: When flying with a tailwind close to the ground, the visual impression tends to modify your perception of airspeed; it may happen that, without realizing it, you:

options.C: Enter a stall.

q248.question: If you are flying into a meteorological headwind, to maintain maximum glide ratio over the ground, compared with the airspeed for maximum glide ratio through the air, you must:

options.C: Seek the lowest sink rate.

q249.question: To maintain maximum glide ratio over the ground, you must fly faster than the speed for maximum glide ratio through the air:

options.C: With a headwind.
options.D: With a tailwind.

q250.question: You meet another wing head-on. Your closing speed is equal to:

options.B: The sum of the two airspeeds.
options.C: Half your airspeed.

q251.question: When crossing another wing, you must assess and react:

options.C: With a headwind, you have more time than usual.

q252.question: Your ground track is perpendicular to the wind direction:

options.A: The central chord of the wing is perpendicular to the wind direction.
options.C: You are drifting.

options.A: The central chord of the wing aligns with the wind direction; this is the weather-vane effect.
options.B: A crab angle must be used if you are heading toward a point that is not on the wind axis.
options.C: The central chord of the wing forms an angle with the ground track.
options.D: The central chord of the wing remains parallel to the ground track.

q254.question: Select the two elements that define the drift angle:

options.B: The direction of the meteorological wind.
options.C: The direction of relative airflow.
options.D: The ground track.

q255.question: Strong dangerous turbulence may be found:

options.A: Leeward of terrain in strong wind.
options.C: In dynamic lift zones.

q256.question: In turbulent conditions it is better to:

options.A: Do not perform maneuvers that require great precision.
options.C: Do not approach the limits of the piloting variables.

q257.question: In turbulent flight, it is better to:

options.A: Do not perform maneuvers that require great precision.
options.B: Avoid playing with excessively low speeds.
options.C: Avoid playing with excessively high speeds.

options.A: The stresses on the structure increase with flight speed.
options.B: You must fly as slowly as possible so as not to fatigue the structure.
options.C: You must fly as fast as possible to leave the area.

q259.question: You are flying a leg of the route where the wind is mostly crosswind relative to your track. You should preferably choose the cumulus clouds:

options.A: Upwind of the route to be followed.
options.B: Downwind of the route to be followed.
options.C: On the route to be followed.

q260.question: Before making a turn, you must:

options.A: Assess the altitude loss caused by the turn and the new trajectory.
options.B: Check that the area where you will be after the turn is clear.
options.C: Go more slowly.
Pay attention to the wind gradient near the terrain.
options.D: Keep enough speed margin to be able to move away from the slope/ridge in an emergency.

q262.question: In ridge soaring, to make better use of lift, fly with an angle of attack:

options.C: For maximum speed.
options.D: For minimum speed.

options.A: Toward the side where the wing rises.
options.C: Toward the side opposite the terrain.

q264.question: In ridge soaring, turning toward the slope is:

options.B: It is better to avoid it.
options.C: Is difficult to control and dangerous because the ground passes by quickly with a tailwind.
options.D: Is what imposes the steepest bank angle for the same turning radius relative to the ground.

options.A: Perfect control of the flight path.
options.C: Be accustomed to the drift effects imposed by the wind.

q266.question: During ridge soaring in turbulent air:

options.B: Fly slightly farther away from the terrain and increase speed.
options.C: Fly as fast as possible.

q267.question: When a pilot uses a thermal bubble to gain altitude:

options.A: He rises at the same speed as the bubble.
options.B: He enters the bubble from its lower part and exits at the top.
options.C: He continuously descends inside the bubble.

q268.question: You manage to get below a large cumulus cloud, observe some ragged cloud fragments, and see a strongly positive variometer reading. You:

options.B: Increase speed to bring the variometer to zero.
options.C: Accelerate toward the clearer side to escape the cloud’s influence.
options.D: Look for sinking air on the windward side of the cloud.

q269.question: During a spiral, your aircraft drifted 3 km in 10 minutes. The wind speed is:

options.C: 18 km/h

q270.question: During a spiral, your aircraft drifted 3 km in 10 minutes. The wind speed is:

options.C: 18 km/h

q271.question: You approach the base of a cumulus:

options.A: There is no risk of cloud suck.
options.B: You may be sucked upward by lift that tends to become stronger.
options.C: The risk is lower as long as the cloud is not a congestus or nimbus type.
options.D: The risk worsens if the base is below the highest ridges.

q272.question: On a beautiful day with lift, when thunderstorms have been forecast, you should become concerned if:

options.C: The cloud shadows on the ground grow quickly.

q273.question: A large isolated cumulus cloud is always harmless:

options.A: False; it may have lift stronger than your ability to escape and may become a cumulonimbus.
options.B: False; it may hide other aircraft.
options.C: True.

q274.question: The passage of a cumulonimbus through your flying sector:

options.A: It invites you to use the lift before the rain arrives.
options.B: It forces you to land immediately.
options.C: May make the landing problematic.

q275.question: After a long flight, it is advisable to relax and prepare the arms and legs for the landing run:

options.C: On final.
options.D: By doing a few movements in the air during the approach.

q276.question: You have a 10 km/h headwind to return to the landing field:

options.A: Try to stay at minimum sink rate to have time to complete the route.
options.B: Fly as fast as possible to have maximum penetration speed.
options.C: Using reference points and managing speed, try to find the best glide angle.

q277.question: An unknown landing field must be inspected:

options.A: In flight, positioning yourself overhead to observe it.
options.C: At the moment of landing.

q278.question: You head toward the landing field with a tailwind at cruising speed and see the nearer edge of the field rising in relation to you:

options.A: You will just reach the field and land with a tailwind.
options.B: You will arrive above the field, turn around, and land into wind.
options.C: Choose a better-positioned alternate landing field.
options.D: Slow down to use the best ground glide ratio due to the tailwind.

q279.question: You are on approach; there is a wing below you:

options.A: You watch it and wait for it, because you must give it right of way.
options.B: You have right of way over the other wing.
options.C: You make a few tight spirals to land sooner.
options.D: You continue your flight, because there are no right-of-way rules during approach.

options.B: Takes the meteorological wind and its possible variations into account.
options.C: Must be adjustable if needed.

q281.question: A good approach includes a final leg:

options.A: Into wind at the far end of the field.
options.B: Into wind at the entry side of the field.
options.C: It all depends on the conditions.

q282.question: Doing 360-degree turns to lose altitude before landing:

options.C: Make you lose sight of reference points.
options.D: Expose you to a collision risk with other approaching wings.

options.A: A reconnaissance at a good altitude: ground wind, problems, planned circuit, and so on.
options.B: S-turns to assess conditions and be precise.
options.C: A final turn not too close to the ground and a stabilized final segment.

q284.question: You are flying an S-shaped approach and your flight path is dropping below the planned descent path. You must:

options.A: Reduce the amplitude of the S-turns.
options.C: If necessary, fly straight toward the field.
options.D: Accelerate so as not to waste time.

q285.question: To be precise in landing, correctly visualizing the slope of the final path is essential. This slope:

options.A: Varies as a function of angle of attack.
options.B: Varies as a function of wind direction and speed.
options.C: Does not depend on the wing's aerodynamic characteristics.

q286.question: You are on final, facing the landing field, and realize you are slightly long and high and may overshoot. You choose to:

options.A: Lose height by putting the wing into a slight deep stall.
options.C: Increase the distance to be flown by changing heading.
options.D: Increase parasite drag by sitting upright.

q287.question: On final approach, if the chosen target moves downward in your field of view, you:

options.C: You must slow down as much as possible.

options.B: Is necessary to maintain an airspeed reserve in case of wind gradient.
options.C: Is dangerous because the aircraft will climb when braking.
options.D: Is necessary to improve maneuverability close to the ground.

options.B: Is dangerous and may lead to a stall.
options.C: Increases the sink rate.
options.D: For safety, requires building speed.

q290.question: Final braking in a strong-wind landing:

options.C: Requires precise modulation.

options.B: Follows the same laws of the air and the same requirements as other air sports.
options.C: Forgives most piloting errors because you practice with a parachute.
options.D: Requires serious training when used as a glider for performance flying, and lighter training in paralpinism when used to descend from mountains.

options.A: Is made on the first or second day of the course after inflating the paraglider a couple of times.
options.B: Is done directly, because of the accident risks on a training slope.
options.C: Requires serious technical training.
options.D: Requires progressive adaptation to exposure and height.

q293.question: Obtaining a paraglider pilot license requires progression:

options.C: That includes a minimum of 40 significant flights.
options.D: That ends with the purchase of your first wing.

q294.question: When receiving a new wing, you must:

options.A: First, test it with one or two inflations on flat ground to check the brakes.
options.B: Then check the wing’s performance at a familiar flying site.
options.C: Add a carabiner to the risers to make gripping easier.

q295.question: The best profile for a takeoff area is one where the pilot can:

options.A: Move away from the terrain quickly.
options.B: Run easily down the slope to reach flying speed.
options.C: Run only a little and launch quickly.
options.D: Stop in case of problems during inflation.

options.A: Is made easier by lower air density.
options.B: Is mountaineering/paralpinism and not free flight.
options.C: Requires a high level of experience.
options.D: Requires no particular mountaineering knowledge.

options.B: Requires running at least at the wing’s minimum-sink speed.
options.C: Requires significant brake input.

q298.question: At takeoff there is 30 km/h of wind, and my wing has a maximum airspeed of 35 km/h:

options.A: I must run at 5 km/h to take off.
options.B: I do not take off because I consider the safety margin insufficient.
options.C: I pull the A-risers as soon as I have taken off.

q299.question: At takeoff, with 20 km/h headwind, the wing may be inflated:

options.B: Facing the slope, with another person securing us so we do not get pulled backward when the wing rises.
options.C: With your back to the slope, turning around to take off.

q300.question: Facing the wing during inflation allows takeoff:

options.A: Without risk in winds over 30 km/h.
options.C: Allows easy control of the wing while it rises.
options.D: Without any assistance in all cases.

q301.question: At takeoff the wind is crosswind (30 degrees, 3 m/s):

options.A: Inflate facing the wind, build speed, and progressively align the run down the slope.
options.B: Run along the fall line while pulling the A-risers.

q302.question: A 30 km/h headwind at takeoff when you are alone:

options.A: Is too much; you should refrain from flying.
options.B: An experienced pilot may take off without problems with their back to the wind.
options.C: You try a few inflations because either you will not be able to manage it, or the wing will come up on its own without needing to run.

q303.question: Setting up the paraglider on the ground depends:

options.A: On the strength and direction of the wind.
options.C: On the paraglider's characteristics.

options.A: Inflating the wing (acceleration and looking forward).
options.B: Stabilizing and controlling the wing (pausing and looking at the wing).
options.C: Takeoff (acceleration and looking forward).

q305.question: The stabilization and control of the wing:

options.A: Is necessary to control the whole wing and detect possible knots in the lines.
options.B: Requires a brief pause in acceleration.
options.C: Is necessary to verify that the newly built aircraft is fit to fly.

q306.question: The acceleration phase after stabilization requires:

options.B: Leaning forward to avoid losing balance.
options.C: Leaning backward to control the wing better.

q307.question: During inflation in strong wind, the wing begins to pull you strongly backward and you risk being dragged:

options.A: Release the brakes and hold on to whatever you can to support yourself.
options.B: Pull both brakes fully to stop yourself.
options.C: Control the wing by pulling one rear riser and moving toward it.
options.D: Flag the wing with the help of rear risers.

q308.question: During inflation, the wing forms a crescent shape. You should:

options.B: Pull the brakes, then hold the A-risers.
options.C: Stop and start again.
options.D: Pull the A-risers.

q309.question: After inflation, your wing has an inflation defect on the right and deviates to the right:

options.A: Run more slowly and pull hard on the right brake.
options.B: Brake vigorously to the right, then turn to the left.
options.C: Follow the wing until the path is corrected using the brakes, then pull the brakes to finish inflating.

q310.question: During stabilization, your wing remains to the left:

options.A: You correct by moving to the right to recover the wing.
options.B: Follow the wing by moving left, gently braking on the right to bring it back to the fall line.
options.C: Maintain speed by pulling the A-risers.
options.D: Accelerate and apply left brake.

q311.question: During stabilization, several left-side cells are closed:

options.A: Pull the right brake and slow down.
options.B: Pull the left front riser to give that side more speed.
options.C: Maintain speed and try to re-inflate with a light brake movement.
options.D: Do not worry; they always end up opening on their own.

q312.question: On the ground, your wing deviates from the planned path:

options.A: You correct by bringing the wing with your body and braking on the side you want to bring it toward.
options.B: Follow the wing to stay centered and bring it back by braking on the side where you want to go.
options.C: You correct with the front risers.

q313.question: Your wing is inflated and stabilized. To take off:

options.A: Continue accelerating while firmly holding the A-risers.
options.B: Accelerate with hands up until takeoff.
options.C: Accelerate with hands up and brake at the moment of takeoff.
options.D: Accelerate while holding the brakes at the cruising-flight position.

options.A: Release the brakes to sit more comfortably.
options.B: You cannot pilot if you are badly seated.
options.C: Visually check that the paraglider is well inflated.
options.D: Focus on your trajectory and on the other pilots flying.

q315.question: The use of A-risers in flight:

options.A: Is not very useful aerodynamically because the wing sinks more than it gains in penetration.
options.C: Creates more problems than solutions.
May cause a collapse of the wing.

q317.question: In flight, with hands up, the brakes are tight and the trailing edge is slightly lowered:

options.A: This improves glide ratio without using the brakes.
options.B: It has little influence on the wing.
options.C: It is better for inflation.
options.D: The brakes must be lengthened and adjusted for the next flight.

q318.question: In flight, if you release the brakes:

options.B: The wing flies at maximum speed.
options.C: They must never be released.

options.A: With the brakes in your hands.
options.C: By symmetrically pulling one or several outer front lines; read your wing’s technical instructions.

options.B: Decreases the glide ratio through the air.
options.C: Increases the wing's maneuverability.

options.B: With a headwind, your glide ratio over the ground increases.
options.C: You descend more quickly and safely.
options.D: Does not damage the wing.

q322.question: In turbulence, you perform big ears:

options.A: Your wing will be more stable.
options.B: Your wing will be more sensitive.
options.C: Is discouraged.
Damages the anchor points of this row of lines.
options.D: May cause a stabilized deep-stall situation.

q324.question: At minimum sink speed, to start a right turn without losing too much speed and risking entry into a flat spin, you must:

options.C: Raise the left brake.

options.A: Must not be done while landing.
options.B: May be vigorous because the risk of stall is zero.
options.C: Is made easier by weight-shift action in the harness.
options.D: Must be banked more steeply as the updraft becomes narrower.

options.C: Allows drift to be assessed.

q327.question: After a piloting error, you notice that the relative wind has almost completely stopped. The wing sinks. To return it as quickly as possible to normal flight, you must:

options.B: Progressively pull the front risers to reduce the angle of attack.
options.C: Release the brakes smoothly to damp the surge.

q328.question: Entering a stall may be dangerous:

options.C: In turbulent conditions.

q329.question: To recover from a symmetrical stall entry:

options.A: Wait for the wing to recover by itself.
options.B: Give sharp pulls on the A-risers.
options.C: Give sharp pulls on the brakes.
options.D: Release the brakes smoothly and damp the surge.

q330.question: You are flying at minimum sink and are about to enter lift:

options.C: Prepare to release the brakes.
options.D: Increase your speed.

q331.question: To remain in dynamic lift near the terrain, you:

options.A: Make 360-degree turns in front of the slope/ridge.
options.B: Pull the brakes down a lot to fly slowly.
options.C: Make wide figure-eights, turning into wind.
options.D: Make wide figure-eights, turning toward the slope.

q332.question: In flight, if you feel turbulence:

options.C: Fly slightly slower than cruise speed.

q333.question: In turbulent flight, to minimize the risks of auto-rotation and turning toward the slope/ridge, you should fly:

options.C: Between minimum sink rate and maximum glide ratio.

q334.question: In ridge soaring, violent turbulence collapses 50% of the wing:

options.A: Raise your arms and build speed to avoid entering a stall.
options.B: Observe the nature of terrain below your feet and prepare for a forced landing.
options.C: Control your trajectory to avoid turning toward the slope while maintaining enough speed to avoid stall entry or autorotation.
options.D: Pull the brake on the closed side to try to reopen it.

q335.question: During ridge soaring at 50 m above the ground, you suddenly sink toward the terrain and the sound of the relative wind decreases. To avoid impact:

options.A: Brake immediately to control the wing better.
options.B: Release the brakes calmly and turn when speed is sufficient to move away from the slope/ridge.
options.C: The situation is unrecoverable; prepare to land on the slope/ridge.

q336.question: Your wing suddenly deflates on the terrain side:

options.A: Re-inflate it firmly while avoiding a turn toward the terrain.
options.B: First try to move away from the terrain while maintaining enough speed before performing the appropriate maneuver to re-inflate the wing.
options.C: Do nothing; the wing re-inflates automatically.

q337.question: You are with your back to the terrain and several left-side cells collapse, causing the wing to turn left:

options.A: If the paraglider has ACPUL certification, let it recover by itself.
options.B: Brake violently to the left to re-inflate the collapsed side.
options.C: Brake symmetrically on both sides.
options.D: You correct to the right to move away from the terrain while maintaining sufficient speed.

q338.question: In turbulent flight, most of your wing’s leading edge collapses. You:

options.C: Raise your hands to achieve maximum speed.

options.A: Release the brakes to accelerate and reinflate.
options.B: Brake on the opposite side to stabilize the trajectory while keeping enough speed.
options.C: Shift your weight to the right side of the harness.
options.D: Make smooth, wide movements with the left brake, with the trajectory stabilized.

q340.question: Your wing is in deep stall; the usual recovery maneuvers are:

options.B: Pull both brakes to put the wing briefly into stall.
options.C: Pull both A-risers.
options.D: If you are close to the ground, prepare for a forced landing without doing anything, since any of the previous maneuvers will pitch you forward and increase speed.

q341.question: The risk of entering a flat spin:

options.A: Increases when pulling one brake strongly in slow flight.
options.B: Increases when pulling one brake strongly in fast flight.
options.C: Varies according to the type of the wing.

q342.question: To recover from a flat spin, you must:

options.A: Raise the brakes smoothly while countering on the outside.
options.B: Brake on the outside of the turn and pull the front riser on the inside of the turn.

q343.question: In flight, your right brake breaks; you can pilot with:

options.A: The left brake and the right rear riser.
options.B: The left brake and the right front riser.
options.C: The two rear risers (symmetry of sensations).
options.D: The A-risers, to land quickly.

options.C: It can be compensated by piloting with the rear risers.

q345.question: You are forced to land on sloping ground:

options.A: Always land uphill, against the slope.
options.B: If the slope is steep, land into wind.
options.C: If the slope is gentle, land into wind.
options.D: If the slope is steep, land cross-slope.

q346.question: You are on final facing the landing field and realize you are slightly high and long, with a risk of overshooting the field:

options.A: Brake the wing progressively to the point of deep stall.
options.B: Lose height by making a 360-degree turn.
options.C: Increase the track by making a small S-turn.
options.D: Pull and release the brakes several times to shake the wing and reduce the glide ratio.

options.B: Is necessary to maintain good airspeed, because wind speed decreases as you approach the ground.
options.C: Is dangerous because the aircraft will climb during a braked final.
options.D: Makes the braking action more effective.

q348.question: To land smoothly when there is no wind:

options.A: You fly the whole final leg with the brakes at hip level.
options.C: You pull the brakes fully at the entry to the field.
options.D: You build speed at the beginning of the final leg.

q349.question: At 5 m above the ground, you realize that you are going to land with a crosswind:

options.A: Make the braked final normally without trying to turn.
options.B: Try to turn so you end up into wind.
options.C: Pull the brake fully to correct.

q350.question: On final, 30 m above the ground, you realize that you are going to land with a tailwind:

options.A: Prepare to brake firmly and run.
options.C: Pull the brakes to enter deep stall.

q351.question: There is 20 km/h of wind at the landing field; you prepare:

options.A: To pull the brakes fully when touching down.
options.B: To brake gently and walk backward with the canopy as it comes down.
options.C: To pull the A-risers to brake smoothly.
options.D: Turn around after landing and hold one or both rear risers so you are not dragged.

options.A: You must look at the wing to make sure it does not collapse.
options.B: You must reduce speed a lot.
options.C: Landing in a turn is discouraged because it increases sink rate and the pilot's lateral swing.
options.D: You must increase speed on final before braking.

q353.question: The landing approach circuit is used:

options.A: Only as training for later passing the exams organized by the F.A.V.L.
options.B: Exclusively for beginners as an assistance maneuver during their first landings.
options.C: As with other aircraft, as a procedure that gives the pilot the greatest chance of making a precise landing.
options.D: According to the selected altitude-loss area and the direction of turn, to indicate to approaching pilots which landing circuit has been chosen.

q354.question: The part of the approach pattern marked with number 1 in the diagram is called:

options.C: Initial.
options.D: Final.

q355.question: The part of the approach pattern marked with number 2 in the diagram is called:

options.C: Initial.
options.D: Final.

q356.question: The part of the approach pattern marked with number 3 in the diagram is called:

options.C: Initial.
options.D: Final.

q357.question: The part of the approach pattern marked with number 4 in the diagram is called:

options.C: Initial.
options.D: Final.

q358.question: When arriving with too much altitude at the landing zone, and before starting the initial leg, you always lose that altitude:

options.C: With turns in the same direction as the chosen approach circuit.
options.D: By making "S" turns.

q359.question: You have decided to fly a left-hand approach circuit. The excess altitude must be lost before the initial leg in the quadrant:

options.C: Lower right.
options.D: Lower left.

q360.question: You have decided to fly a right-hand approach circuit. The excess altitude must be lost before the initial leg in the quadrant:

options.C: Lower right.
options.D: Lower left.

q361.question: The following four diagrams show the standard approach pattern in no wind (dotted line), and a corrected circuit suitable for the situation that actually occurs at the landing field (solid line). There is in fact no wind on the field, and when moving from the initial leg to base leg you realize that you are slightly below the ideal altitude. The change you make is the one shown in figure:

options.C: C.
options.D: D.

q362.question: The following four diagrams show the standard approach pattern in no wind (dotted line), and a corrected circuit suitable for the situation that actually occurs at the landing field (solid line). There is in fact no wind on the field, and when moving from the initial leg to base leg you realize that you are slightly above the ideal altitude. The change you make is the one shown in figure:

options.C: C.
options.D: D.

q363.question: The following four diagrams show the standard approach pattern in no wind (dotted line), and a corrected circuit suitable for the situation that actually occurs at the landing field (solid line). On the field, the wind is 10 to 15 km/h. The change you make is the one shown in figure:

options.C: C.
options.D: D.

q364.question: The following four diagrams show the standard approach pattern in no wind (dotted line), and a corrected circuit suitable for the situation that actually occurs at the landing field (solid line). On the field, the wind is 25 to 30 km/h. The change you make is the one shown in figure:

options.C: C.
options.D: D.

q365.question: You arrive first at the landing zone, followed by two other pilots 50 m behind you horizontally. There is no wind, there are no obstacles at any edge of field, and there are no specific landing rules for the site. Since you are the first pilot:

options.A: You may choose a right-hand or left-hand circuit and a final leg in any direction, since there is no wind.
options.B: You may freely choose the direction of the final leg, but must fly a left-hand approach circuit.
options.C: You may freely choose a left-hand or right-hand approach, but the final leg must be across the valley axis.
options.D: You must fly a left-hand approach circuit, but the final leg must be across the valley axis.

q366.question: You arrive first at the landing zone, followed by two other pilots 50 m behind you horizontally. There is no wind, and you begin a left-hand approach circuit with final toward the west. When entering base leg, you discover that there is actually a 2 to 5 km/h wind from the east, and therefore:

options.A: You continue with the chosen circuit, but expect your final-leg trajectory to have a shallower slope.
options.B: You immediately interrupt the chosen circuit, head toward the leeward side of target, and land into wind.
options.C: You continue with the chosen circuit, but expect your final-leg trajectory to have a steeper slope.
options.D: You treat final as base leg and ultimately land with a crosswind.

q367.question: You arrive second at the landing zone, with 50 m horizontal spacing from the first and third pilots. There is a light west wind (2 to 5 km/h) and no nearby obstacles. The first pilot begins a right-hand approach circuit and plans to land downwind. You:

options.A: Follow the pilot ahead of you, flying the same approach circuit to avoid a collision risk.
options.B: Fly a right-hand approach circuit but plan to land into wind.
options.C: Fly a left-hand approach circuit and also land downwind.
options.D: Fly a left-hand approach circuit and land into wind.

q368.question: You are flying your approach circuit to a landing field where the windsock indicates a light breeze from the west. On final, you discover that the wind has shifted and is now coming from the east at 5 to 10 km/h. You:

options.A: Immediately make a 180-degree turn and land into wind.
options.B: Continue as planned, flying more braked to compensate for the tailwind and reduce ground speed.
options.C: Continue as planned, sit upright, fly without braking, and flare slightly earlier for landing.
options.D: Continue as planned and, with successive regular pulls on both brakes, try to make the wing lose height vertically.

options.A: The trajectory has a shallower slope and the landing speed is lower.
options.B: The trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.C: The trajectory has a shallower slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.D: The trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is lower.

options.A: The trajectory has a shallower slope and the landing speed is lower.
options.B: The trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.C: The trajectory has a shallower slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.D: The trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is lower.

q371.question: You land with a 30 km/h headwind. As soon as your feet touch the ground:

options.A: Pull the brakes down as much as possible so the wing immediately stops flying.
options.B: Turn 180 degrees and pull the brakes down as much as possible so the wing immediately stops flying.
options.C: Pull one brake as much as possible so the wing falls onto its leading edge and stops producing lift.
options.D: Turn 180 degrees and pull the B or C risers so the wing immediately stops flying.
